text.title: Benefits Realization Rules and Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Rules for ownership, baseline discipline, attribution, agentic tracking, and avoiding the common failure modes that make benefit tracking theatre.

rule.statement: The benefit owner is a named business stakeholder with operational levers post-handover; the PM is never the benefit owner.
rule.rationale: PMs have no authority over adoption, process change, or operational incentives after project close; assigning ownership to the PM creates accountability without power, guaranteeing the benefit is never tracked.

rule.statement: Baseline measurements must be taken before go-live; if the baseline cannot be established, flag it as an open task (baseline=null), not a zero.
rule.rationale: A zero baseline implies the current state is zero, which is rarely true; null + open-task is honest and triggers the measurement activity before launch.

rule.statement: Sponsor sign-off on benefit definitions — metric, baseline, target, owner, measurement source — is a project initiation gate; "we'll figure out metrics later" is a known failure mode.
rule.rationale: Benefits defined post-launch are shaped by the actual outcomes observed, not by the investment thesis; they cannot serve as evidence of ROI.

rule.statement: Declare the attribution method upfront (cohort, before/after with controls, holdout, qualitative); default to "qualitative" rather than fabricating causality from correlation.
rule.rationale: Multiple concurrent initiatives make clean attribution impossible without deliberate experimental design; admitting qualitative attribution is honest. Asserting causality without design is misleading and damages trust when challenged.

antipattern.description: Agents or analysts asserting benefit causality from correlation in post-launch data without a declared attribution method.
antipattern.reason: Attribution hallucination overstates the project's impact, inflates reported ROI, and corrupts future investment decisions by rewarding projects that benefited from coincident market or operational improvements.

rule.statement: The benefit-author agent must reject any benefit entry that lacks metric + baseline + target + owner + metric_source; null baseline is permitted but must flag an open measurement task.
rule.rationale: Incomplete entries are accepted and abandoned; rejection at authoring time forces the conversation about measurability before resources are committed.

rule.statement: The RAG rubric is deterministic: GREEN if actual >= 90% of expected curve, YELLOW 70-89%, RED below 70%; the reporting agent must not soften RED without an explicit sponsor-override note.
rule.rationale: Narrative agents have a politeness bias toward GREEN; a hard rule removes the soft option and ensures sponsors see accurate status even when it is uncomfortable.

rule.statement: Recompute portfolio ROI annually using realized measurement data, not original business-case forecasts; this is the feedback loop that calibrates future investment decisions.
rule.rationale: Organizations that compare actuals to forecasts learn to forecast better; those that only use original forecasts systematically over-invest in project types that reliably under-deliver.
